text: The video shows a direct inguinal hernia located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon initiates the operation by creating a preperitoneal flap, fully exposing the hernia space for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs a sweeping dissection from top to bottom, further exposing the hernia space and separating tissue layers.
The hernia contents, consisting of preperitoneal adipose tissue, are visible protruding through the direct hernia space.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal sac from the hernia contents, reducing them back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon exposes Cooper's ligament, ensuring adequate dissection within the surgical field.
The surgeon evaluates the indirect space, inspecting the area for any hernia contents or anatomical abnormalities.
The surgeon carefully dissects a cord lipoma from the vas deferens and testicular vessels, revealing the absence of an indirect hernia in the surgical field.
The surgeon deploys a mesh into the preperitoneal space after exposing all potential hernia spaces and completing the dissection.
The self-fixating mesh is positioned within the preperitoneal space, adhering to the surrounding tissue without the need for sutures or tacks.
The surgeon positions the self-fixating mesh to cover all potential hernia spaces within the preperitoneal area, ensuring complete coverage without the need for sutures or tacks.
The surgeon sutures the preperitoneal space closed, completing the operation.
